narrative:
Verdant Health Advisory is a five-person healthcare consultancy founded
in 2018, working for a regional health system, an independent physician
group, and an ambulatory surgery joint venture. Its engagements are the
unglamorous middle of healthcare operations: which service lines earn
their keep, whether physician compensation survives a fair-market-value
review, and where the revenue cycle leaks.

The share is modern and mostly clean, with one deliberate exception. The
health system's contracting office prints, signs, and scans everything,
and its scanner produces images with no text layer whatsoever. Those
consents and executed statements of work are in the share as pictures of
documents, which is precisely the case an extraction pipeline cannot
bluff its way through.

Tone is measured and compliance-aware. Consultants write for an audience
that includes a general counsel, so nothing asserts a conclusion the
analysis does not support, and fair-market-value language is used
carefully rather than decoratively. Findings lead; recommendations
follow and are labeled as such. Staff use first names internally and full
names and titles with the client. One client-side contact moved from the
physician group to the health system partway through the relationship,
and documents should place her at the right employer for their date.
instructions:
Author every document briefed in `docs`, as JSON matching the deliverable
schema:

  {"schema_id": "orgsmith/authoring-deliverable@1",
   "work_order_id": "wo:author:0002",
   "docs": [{"schema_id": "orgsmith/docir@1", "doc_id": "d:....",
             "blocks": [...]}, ...]}

Block kinds: heading (text, level), paragraph (text), list (items),
table (header, rows), sigblock (signers = person ids from the brief).

Hard rules:
- One DocIR per brief; doc_ids must match the work order exactly.
- Every fact id briefed for a doc MUST appear as a literal placeholder
  {{fact:<id>}} at least once in that doc's text, where <id> is the
  briefed id verbatim including its `f:` prefix (e.g.
  {{fact:f:E-2019-001.start}}). You do not know fact values; never write
  a number, date, or name in place of a placeholder.
- Use only placeholders for fact ids briefed on that document.
- Do not invent people, organizations, addresses, amounts, or dates.
  People and their titles come from the briefs; the org narrative sets
  tone only.
- Every entry in a doc's `mentions` list must appear VERBATIM (the exact
  `surface` string) somewhere in that doc's text. Sigblock signers count
  as mentions of themselves. Short surfaces are nicknames: work them into
  prose naturally ("...as {{nickname}} noted..."), alongside, not instead
  of, the person's full name.
- If a doc carries `engagement_position`, honor it: it states where the
  document sits in its engagement's timeline. Do not describe progress or a
  phase inconsistent with it, and do not state the engagement's start or
  end date (those are fact placeholders, not yours to write).
- If a doc carries `firm_digest`, it is the ONLY firm history you may treat
  as established as of this document's date. Reference clients only through
  the placeholders it names; claim no engagement it does not list; present
  nothing later than this document as already true.
- Write plain, era-appropriate business prose in the org's voice.

docs.title: Firm Overview

authors.name: Laura Brown
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Laura founded the practice and still reads every deliverable before it leaves the office, mostly hunting for sentences that claim more than the analysis earned. She writes cover letters herself in a measured register, assuming general counsel is in the room even when counsel is not on the distribution, and she is unsentimental about telling a client that the work does not support the conclusion they wanted. Her habit is to lead with what the firm found, then set recommendations apart and label them plainly so nobody can quote a suggestion as a finding. Internally she is warm and first-name; with clients she uses full names and titles without exception, and expects the same of everyone.

participants.name: Laura Brown
participants.title: Managing Partner
participants.persona: Laura founded the practice and still reads every deliverable before it leaves the office, mostly hunting for sentences that claim more than the analysis earned. Internally she is warm and first-name; with clients she uses full names and titles without exception, and expects the same of everyone.

participants.name: Richard Marsh
participants.title: Engagement Manager
participants.persona: Richard keeps the engagements moving: scope, schedule, who owes whom what, and the long tail of executed paperwork that never comes back on the first ask. He writes in a clean administrative voice, confirms commitments in writing the same day, and is unfailingly polite about following up for a third time. He has made a personal study of which client offices still handle everything on paper, and he plans around them rather than complaining about them. Internally he is the one who knows which contact currently sits where, and he corrects a misaddressed draft before it goes out.

docs.firm_digest: As of this document's date the firm has begun 1 client engagement(s) across 2020. Reference clients ONLY through these placeholders, never by inventing a name: {{fact:f:E-2020-001.client}}. Present nothing that post-dates this document as established, and claim no engagement not listed here.

docs.title: Onboarding: Barbara Freeman

authors.name: Laura Brown
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Laura founded the practice and still reads every deliverable before it leaves the office, mostly hunting for sentences that claim more than the analysis earned. She writes cover letters herself in a measured register, assuming general counsel is in the room even when counsel is not on the distribution, and she is unsentimental about telling a client that the work does not support the conclusion they wanted. Her habit is to lead with what the firm found, then set recommendations apart and label them plainly so nobody can quote a suggestion as a finding. Internally she is warm and first-name; with clients she uses full names and titles without exception, and expects the same of everyone.

participants.name: Barbara Freeman
participants.title: Analyst
participants.persona: Barbara came into the practice from an operational background and still asks the naive question that turns out to be the right one. She drafts cleanly and quickly, expects a principal to mark it up, and treats the markup as the point rather than an insult. Her instinct is to over-qualify a finding, which the principals push back on, so her later drafts read more confidently than her early ones. She is scrupulous about noting where a client's data ran out and where an inference began.

docs.guidance: Internal onboarding record welcoming a new hire to the firm: a short welcome, the person's role and where they sit in the practice, and first-period logistics and expectations. Name the new employee. 2-4 short paragraphs, no sigblock.

docs.title: Onboarding: Joshua Dougherty

authors.name: Laura Brown
authors.title: Managing Partner
authors.persona: Laura founded the practice and still reads every deliverable before it leaves the office, mostly hunting for sentences that claim more than the analysis earned. She writes cover letters herself in a measured register, assuming general counsel is in the room even when counsel is not on the distribution, and she is unsentimental about telling a client that the work does not support the conclusion they wanted. Her habit is to lead with what the firm found, then set recommendations apart and label them plainly so nobody can quote a suggestion as a finding. Internally she is warm and first-name; with clients she uses full names and titles without exception, and expects the same of everyone.

participants.name: Joshua Dougherty
participants.title: Analyst
participants.persona: Joshua builds most of the models the advisory work rests on and is happiest in a workbook, reconciling one source against another until they agree or he can say exactly why they do not. He writes spare, functional prose, strongest in table captions and methodology notes, and he tends to let an exhibit carry the argument instead of narrating it. He will not release a figure he cannot reproduce from raw inputs, and he says so when asked to move faster than that allows. In review he catches the arithmetic and the definitional mismatch that everyone else read past.
